steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: Hensley concluded the complementary products' advantage 'may be due to an outlier in the complementary product data.' The evidence must show that one product pair is driving the whole category's higher rate — and that removing it erases the advantage.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — Although consumers were more likely to purchase complementary extended brands than substitutable extended brands, the difference in purchasing probability reduced to zero when tortilla chips and salsa were removed from consideration.: Complementary extended brands had higher purchasing rates overall, but the gap vanished once that single pair was excluded, confirming it was the outlier. ✓
  • Choice B — Although consumers purchased complementary extended brands more frequently than substitutable extended brands, removing ultra-pasteurized milk and raw milk from the data led to an equal purchasing frequency of complementary and substitutable products.: Points to the wrong category; Hensley's conclusion is about complementary products. ✗
  • Choice C — Although consumers were about equally likely to purchase complementary extended brands and substitutable extended brands, removing tortilla chips and salsa from the data increased the likelihood that consumers would purchase complementary extended brands.: Contradicts the observed advantage for complementary products that Hensley was explaining. ✗
  • Choice D — Although consumers purchased substitutable extended brands more often than complementary ones, the removal of ultra-pasteurized milk and raw milk from the data caused the two product types to be purchased at equal rates.: Has substitutable brands outperforming complementary ones, the opposite of what the study found. ✗
That is the correct answer.
